spec_reconstruction.domain: Current, in-force federal board-level obligations for Category I U.S. GSIB bank holding companies and their insured depository institution subsidiaries.
spec_reconstruction.unit_of_analysis: Individual board-level obligation, with source, duty, actor, entity tier, legal status, committee/full-board locus, director-status distinction, evidence artifact, and reform treatment.
structural_axes: parent bank holding company versus insured depository institution subsidiary
Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, CFPB, and interagency source authority
binding regulation, enforceable guideline, supervisory guidance, examination procedure, and nonbinding resource
full board versus risk, audit, compensation, nominating/governance, compliance, technology/cyber, and other committees
current in-force obligation versus proposed, rescinded, nonbinding, or excluded source
approval, review, oversight, reporting, minute notation, escalation, and certification duties
actor_classes_required: full board of Category I GSIB bank holding company
full board of insured depository institution subsidiary
source_universe: Federal Reserve Regulation YY, Regulation Y, Regulation Q, SR letters, CA letters, LFI and board-effectiveness guidance
OCC regulations, appendices, bulletins, heightened standards, BSA and security-program rules
FDIC regulations, part 360 CIDI resolution rules, part 363 audit rules, part 326 BSA rules, safety-and-soundness and information-security guidelines
CFPB examination manuals, CMR, CMR-IT, fair-lending, UDAAP, and service-provider oversight materials
interagency guidance and standards for stress testing, third-party risk, information security, BSA/AML, incentive compensation, and safety and soundness
coverage_areas: consumer compliance, fair lending, UDAAP, and CFPB examination expectations
board composition, independence, committee structure, and director status
spec_reconstruction.audience_use: Bank directors, counsel, and policymakers seeking a working policy-reform inventory of duplicative, overlapping, or disproportionately burdensome board-level requirements.
spec_reconstruction.form_depth_expectations: Granular, citation-specific, extensive 40- to 60-page working document with precise actor mapping, current-law calibration, source hierarchy, and reform-actionable overlap analysis.

material_omissions: The report is a strong working draft but shorter and less exhaustive than the requested 40- to 60-page inventory.
BSA/AML coverage omits some SAR-specific board notification and director-notification rules, such as OCC SAR board-notice requirements and agency analogues.
Consumer compliance coverage relies heavily on CFPB examination procedures and is thin on Federal Reserve consumer compliance guidance such as SR 08-8/CA 08-11.
Information-security coverage omits or underemphasizes the Federal Reserve state-member-bank appendix analogue while covering OCC, FDIC, and BHC codifications.
Nominating/governance committee obligations are thin; board composition and independence are covered, but succession, nominations, and committee-governance duties are not systematically inventoried.
Inside/non-independent director distinctions are mostly handled by negative implication from outside/independent standards rather than mapped as distinct actor classes.

results.reason: The current eCFR rule supports the BHC board risk committee, independent chair, risk-management expert, and risk-policy review claims.
The rule supports annual board liquidity risk tolerance approval, board policy review, contingency funding plan approval, and quarterly board/risk committee reporting.
The liquidity stress-testing and buffer provisions support the report's stress-testing obligations, with board role correctly tied to broader liquidity governance.
The capital-plan and stress-capital-buffer rule supports the report's capital planning and distribution-governance discussion.
Federal Reserve capital-planning guidance supports heightened Category I governance, controls, scenario, projection, and board-reporting expectations.
Advanced-approaches provisions support board-approved implementation plans and internal audit reporting to the board or board committee, though the report could cite narrower sections.
The resolution-plan rules support board approval and minute notation before covered-company plan submission.
The CIDI resolution rule supports IDI board approval/delegation and minute notation for covered insured depository institution resolution submissions.
Federal Reserve recovery-planning guidance supports the report's recovery-planning governance and board/senior-management oversight treatment.
The report correctly treats OCC recovery-planning guidelines as rescinded and not a current in-force board obligation.
FDICIA part 363 supports independent audit committee requirements, including outside-director and independence-of-management standards.
OCC Heightened Standards support the covered-bank risk-governance framework, board oversight, risk appetite approval, independent directors, and escalation duties.

results.citation: 12 CFR part 30 appendix B; 12 CFR part 364 appendix B; 12 CFR part 225 appendix F
results.reason: The interagency information-security guidelines support board or committee approval, oversight, and management-report review obligations.
OCC security-program rules support board designation/oversight and annual security-officer reporting reflected in minutes.
The agency BSA rules support written BSA compliance programs approved by the board and noted in board minutes.

results.citation: SR 23-4 / 2023 Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships
results.reason: The guidance supports third-party lifecycle, responsibility-not-outsourced, board/senior-management oversight, and risk-based critical-activity concepts.
Revised model-risk guidance supports board and senior-management oversight, risk-based tailoring, validation, controls, and governance statements.
The CFPB procedures support board and management oversight, board-minutes review, CMS components, and service-provider oversight as examination expectations.
The cited CFPB IT examination procedures support the report's use of CMR-IT as an operational-resilience and consumer-compliance examination source.

results.citation: Interagency due diligence guide for fintech relationships
results.reason: I did not verify this nonbinding-resource citation against a primary source during the sample; the report labels it peripheral and nonbinding.

actor_mapping.note: The report maps most duties to BHC board, IDI board, risk committee, audit committee, compensation committee, board or appropriate committee, and management-with-board-reporting actors. Nominating/governance and inside/non-independent director classes are thinner.

binding_vs_guidance.note: The report consistently separates binding regulations, enforceable guidelines, supervisory guidance, examination procedures, excluded proposals, and rescinded sources.

reform_usefulness.note: The report gives concrete reform levers, including de-duplicating approvals, separating approval from oversight, harmonizing independence terminology, subsidiary-board reliance, and materiality-based reporting.

D1_coverage.evidence: Category I GSIB boards face a dense board-governance stack built from at least five overlapping layers
D1_coverage.rationale: The report covers nearly all requested domains and actor categories with source/duty/actor/status tables and detailed obligation cards. It is not exemplary because several current-law cells are thin or missing, including SAR board-notification rules, SR 08-8 consumer-compliance guidance, some information-security analogues, and nominating/governance committee detail.

D2_integrity.evidence: The inventory was built from four source categories.
D2_integrity.rationale: The sampled citation base is mostly sound and uses many official URLs. The score is held at 3 because some references are broad part-level citations, one peripheral citation was not verified, and several board-role statements are inferential rather than direct pin-cited obligations.

D3_accuracy.evidence: Proposed rules are not counted as current in-force obligations.
D3_accuracy.rationale: The report is generally current and accurately avoids treating proposed or rescinded materials as binding obligations. It is not a 4 because some current obligations are omitted and a few indirect board roles need more precise legal calibration.

D4_sludge_analysis.evidence: distinguish nondelegable board duties from management tasks
D4_sludge_analysis.rationale: The reform analysis is concrete and action-oriented, with clear duplication categories, burden coding, source-by-source implementation notes, and practical levers for consolidation, reliance, materiality thresholds, and guidance discipline.

D5_structure.evidence: This appendix converts the domain inventory into granular obligation cards.
D5_structure.rationale: The report is well structured with domain chapters, matrices, detailed obligation cards, source register, calendar model, and coding key. It falls short of a top score because it is still a draft-level inventory rather than a fully exhaustive 40- to 60-page legal workpaper with complete pin cites for every duty.

D6_calibration.evidence: It distinguishes binding regulations, enforceable guidelines, supervisory guidance, and examination expectations.
D6_calibration.rationale: The report is unusually transparent about legal status, exclusions, rescinded materials, proposed rules, examination expectations, and counsel-validation needs. It avoids most false precision and guidance-as-law blending.

strengths: D1: Broad domain coverage includes capital, liquidity, resolution, recovery, audit, cyber, third-party risk, model risk, incentive compensation, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, and director status.
D2: The source register uses many official eCFR, Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, and CFPB references, and sampled primary citations mostly support the claims.
D4: The duplication analysis is directly reform-actionable, especially on parent-subsidiary duplication, approval proliferation, committee overlap, and guidance-driven hardening.
D5: The detailed obligation cards provide useful fields such as legal status, board actor, duty summary, evidence artifact, overlap issue, and potential reform treatment.
D6: The report clearly excludes proposals and rescinded materials and repeatedly distinguishes binding law from guidance and examination expectations.
weaknesses: D1: The report is not fully comprehensive; SAR board-notification rules, some agency analogues, SR 08-8 consumer-compliance guidance, and nominating/governance committee duties need more work.
D2: Some citations are too broad, especially references to entire CFR parts rather than precise sections or subsections.

D3: A few board-role statements are inferential, such as quantitative liquidity standards and some model/AI governance references, and should be tied more explicitly to primary text or labeled as inference.
D5: The report is a strong working draft but still below the requested 40- to 60-page, all-obligation legal inventory depth.
D1: Inside and non-independent director distinctions are not independently inventoried, even though outside and independent-director standards are covered well.
prioritized_remediation: Add a complete agency-by-agency BSA/SAR subsection covering board approval, SAR notification, non-suspect director notice, and director-specific obligations.
Expand consumer compliance coverage beyond CFPB examination procedures to include Federal Reserve and prudential consumer-compliance guidance and any board-relevant agency materials.
Replace broad source labels such as '12 CFR part 217' with exact section and subsection pin cites for each obligation card.
Add a dedicated nominating/governance and succession-planning chapter, separating binding disclosure or committee requirements from supervisory board-composition expectations.

Complete an agency analogue crosswalk for information security, safety-and-soundness, BSA, and resolution-planning rules across national banks, state member banks, state nonmember banks, BHCs, and FDIC-supervised IDIs.
narrative.reliance_recommendation: This is useful as a reform-oriented working draft and board-counsel issue map, but it should not be treated as a complete legal inventory until counsel fills the missing cells and verifies every pin cite.
